metadata.title: Exploring Customers’ Luxury Consumption in Restaurants: A Combined Method of Topic Modeling and Three-Factor Theory

abstract: This study explores customers' perceptions and underlying factors related to luxury consumption in restaurants. Although many studies have explored customers' consumption of luxury goods, very few of these studies involved luxury hospitality services. Furthermore, hospitality literature has rarely discussed the emerging identification of inconspicuous consumption in luxury. By applying topic modeling to analyze online customer reviews, the current study identifies the essential elements of visiting luxury restaurants. Moreover, it elicits the asymmetric role of the identified factors in accelerating overall customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction through impact-asymmetry analysis, which adopts the three-factor theory. Findings suggest that many inconspicuous factors exist in luxury consumption and that the mechanisms that affect satisfaction differ among a satisfier, a dissatisfier, and a hybrid. The acknowledgment of the asymmetric effects will help practitioners in luxury restaurants enhance their understandings of customer perceptions and efficiently improve service management and marketing.

paragraphs: The global luxury market has grown at an unprecedented rate (Yang & Mattila, 2017). It recorded a 5% growth in 2018, including a 6% increase in fine dining and a 7% increase in luxury cruises (D'Arpizio et al., 2019). Moreover, luxury brands have extended to the masstige market, and luxury consumption has become democratized (Thomsen et al., 2020). Likewise, luxury consumption in the hospitality industry has become more accessible and affordable than before; therefore, customers who are not affluent can consume a luxury service occasionally. For example, Starbucks launched a new roastery and shops with a luxury brand named "Starbucks Reserve" to penetrate a luxury segment of coffee (Lerman, 2015).

As luxury brands are becoming available to a broad range of customers, the concept of luxury in modern society has changed (Chandon et al., 2016). Scholars have identified a new type of inconspicuous consumption in luxury regarding the meaningfulness that individuals may have in their consumption experience, as opposed to the traditional conspicuous consumption concerning social class and status that people crave to reveal (Eckhardt et al., 2015). This "unconventional luxury" is subtle and complex because it features a subjective and experiential aspect of luxury even in daily life (Kreuzer et al., 2020). Hence, managers should exert an effort to understand the emerging trend of luxury consumption to satisfy their customers.

Notably, the consumption of luxury hospitality services is different from that of luxury products (Yang et al., 2016;Yang & Mattila, 2016) and cannot be fully explained by typical motives for conspicuous consumption of luxury goods. For example, people can only consume luxury restaurant services in a less exposed manner (Chen & Peng, 2018;Kang, 2018). However, prior luxury research focused on luxury goods that can be possessed and signal owners' social status (Cristini et al., 2017;Hwang & Hyun, 2013). Despite a boost and superior performance in the market, relatively little is known about luxury hospitality services (Chen & Peng, 2018;Yang & Mattila, 2016). Thus, hospitality researchers can contribute to the present luxury research by investigating various aspects, and the objective of this study is to explore customers' perceptions of luxuries in restaurants, reflecting recently extended understandings of luxury, including conspicuous and inconspicuous consumption.

The study uses online reviews posted on Yelp.com to achieve the objective. Scholars have extensively investigated customer-generated social media content to obtain a deep understanding of customers' emotions, attitudes, and perceptions (Lee et al., 2017). Moreover, recent luxury service research demonstrates that luxury service consumption and luxury service experience significantly influence customers' positive word-of-mouth (WOM) on luxury hospitality purchases (Yang & Mattila, 2017). We consider online reviews a great source to uncover the multifaceted concepts of luxury hidden in customers' minds. Moreover, the present study introduces an innovative mixed-method approach combining topic modeling and the three-factor theory (Kano, 1984) to delineate the asymmetric influences of luxury service attributes. Specifically, the study applies the impact-asymmetry analysis to the result of the topic modeling and discovers the dynamic aspects of luxury service factors on customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction. Through this theoretical and methodological lens, this work proposes and examines two research questions as follows:

Research Questions 1: What are the underlying factors or perceptions related to the luxury service consumption experience? Research Questions 2: How differently do these underlying factors play roles in influencing customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction?

This study contributes to research by extending our understanding of customers' perceptions from a holistic perspective of conspicuous and inconspicuous luxury consumption. Another contribution is the provision of an integrated methodological framework by using topic modeling and impact-asymmetry analysis to further examine the association of findings.

Kwon et al. - 2022 - Exploring Customers’ Luxury Consumption in Restaurants A Combined Method of Topic Modeling and Thre
paragraphs: The term "luxury" has various definitions and concepts (Wiedmann et al., 2009). Historically, luxury was perceived as a danger of excessive desire to pursue beyond what is needed, and sumptuary laws restricted its consumption to maintain a distinction among social classes (Cristini et al., 2017). Given that Veblen's (1899) seminal work identified luxury goods' conspicuous consumption, as customers attempt to display their financial strength to other people, studies have explored luxury considering social relationships among people. This social motivation denotes the interpersonal aspect of luxury, that is, the desire for luxury brand consumption in the social context to impress others (Wiedmann et al., 2009). For example, scholars have argued that "bandwagon effects" are valid in luxury markets, explaining that people purchase goods to follow others (Kastanakis & Balabanis, 2012). By contrast, "snob effects" present the phenomenon that customers lose the desire to possess luxury goods when they become common (Kiatkawsin & Han, 2019). Similarly, Rucker and Galinsky (2008) demonstrated that customers with relatively low status tend to buy luxury products that signal status.

Yet, in line with the change in the luxury market where luxury brands have penetrated the mass market to attract more people, the definition and standard of luxury have become complex (Chandon et al., 2016;Han et al., 2010;Kapferer & Laurent, 2016). Unlike the traditional conspicuous consumption of luxury, scholars have identified a new type of inconspicuous consumption of luxury in which customers do not publicly display their social status (Wu et al., 2017). According to Eckhardt et al. (2015), several reasons, including the diluted signaling ability of a luxury product or customers' pursuit of self-pleasure, have caused inconspicuous consumption. Recent research has proposed various frameworks, including meaningfulness (Kauppinen-Räisänen et al., 2019), self-identity (Makkar & Yap, 2018), hedonic escapism (Holmqvist et al., 2020), and interpersonal interaction (Kreuzer et al., 2020), to address the concept of inconspicuous consumption.

Conceptualizing luxury in a hospitality and tourism setting is even more difficult. Despite some cues (e.g., a star rating system in a hotel brand or a typology of quick service, mid-scale, and upscale in restaurants) to identify luxury, the concept is unclear because firms create luxury services by using their criteria (Harkison et al., 2018). Present literature tends to dig less into the conceptualization of luxury and typically does not explicitly define luxury with only some exceptions. For instance, Yang and Mattila (2016) described luxury restaurants offer a high quality of food and service, unique presentations, and sophisticated physical surroundings. Correia et al. (2020) noted that the definition of luxury tourism is more related to customers' behavior than a destination. However, various facets of inconspicuous consumption that often occur in the hospitality and tourism industry are missing in the conceptualization. Hospitality and tourism articles require absorbing the recent achievement of understanding the contemporary luxury from other disciplines, as stated above. Moreover, the conceptualization of luxury should be customer-centric, rather than attribute-centric. For instance, although a considerable price premium is an essential attribute of luxury, it is not always linked with luxury, as customers' reference points of price, income level, and social status are all different (Chang & Ko, 2017;Kiatkawsin & Han, 2019). Scholars have also agreed that the concept of luxury is extremely subjective, and customers' experiences and expectations define the standard of luxury (Harkison et al., 2018). Consolidating the notion from previous literature, the present study defines "luxury" in restaurants as food, services, and atmosphere that customers perceive to be exclusively desirable and thus are worthy of commanding a premium price, whether conspicuous or inconspicuous (Heine, 2012;Ko et al., 2019;Tynan et al., 2010).

Considerable hospitality research on luxury has investigated value-based empirical models, including functional, financial, hedonic, relational, and symbolic value, to understand how customers' luxury value influences their buying behavior (e.g., Chen & Peng, 2018;Kang, 2018;Wu & Yang, 2018;Yang & Mattila, 2016). Most studies have shown that the effect of symbolic value on customers' behavioral intentions is invalid, except for Chen and Peng (2018) who reported a positive relationship between the two constructs. Some articles have also examined the centric role of conspicuous consumption (e.g., Kiatkawsin & Han, 2019;Yang et al., 2016;Yang & Mattila, 2017). In the meantime, Hwang and Hyun (2013) investigated the effect of a sense of nostalgia in luxury restaurants. Despite these ongoing efforts, most studies depend on the factors derived from research on customer value. Meanwhile, noble discussions about various customer perceptions, including the inconspicuous consumption, remain underexplored. Table 1 shows a summary of the recent luxury literature in hospitality management.

paragraphs: Extending Herzberg's (1968) two-factor theory, Kano (1984) suggested the three-factor theory that divides service attributes into three different categories: a satisfier, a dissatisfier, and a hybrid. This theory emphasizes the asymmetric influence of service attributes on overall customer satisfaction. The satisfier is linearly related only to delight with extraordinarily high satisfaction and does not influence dissatisfaction even with frustration. By contrast, the dissatisfier is a minimum requirement that merely prevents dissatisfaction and does not lead to satisfaction even when the service or product exceeded customers' expectations. The hybrid is a factor that has a linear relationship from dissatisfaction to satisfaction as performance improves (Figure 1). In summary, the hybrid has an asymmetric relationship with satisfaction, and the satisfier and dissatisfier have asymmetric relationships with satisfaction. The low performance of the dissatisfier has a greater influence on satisfaction than high performance, whereas the high performance of the satisfier has a greater effect on satisfaction than low performance (Füller & Matzler, 2008).

The three-factor theory is known as a robust approach to understanding the complex relationships between factors and satisfaction (Slevitch & Oh, 2010). Initially, scholars used a qualitative approach to distinguish three categories. For example, they asked customers to classify attributes as satisfied or dissatisfied (Lai & Hitchcock, 2017). Later, they learned to use quantitative methods to test asymmetric effects (Mikulić & Prebežac, 2011). In hospitality and tourism literature, Back (2012) used impact-range performance analysis to explore the asymmetric influences of quality on satisfaction. Lai and Hitchcock (2017) applied the threefactor theory to identify service quality attributes' effects on satisfaction. Bi et al. (2020) further revealed that the asymmetric impacts of attributes on satisfaction depend on the types of hotels, travelers, and regions.

sections.preview_tail: In hospitality and tourism literature, Back (2012) used impact-range performance analysis to explore the asymmetric influences of quality on satisfaction. Lai and Hitchcock (2017) applied the threefactor theory to identify service quality attributes' effects on satisfaction. Bi et al. (2020) further revealed that the asymmetric impacts of attributes on satisfaction depend on the types of hotels, travelers, and regions.

sections.header_raw: Machine Learning Analysis: Word Embedding and Topic Modeling
sections.header_norm: machine learning analysis word embedding and topic modeling
paragraphs: To detect similar words to "luxury" in the context, the study employed the word embedding technique by using the Word2vec algorithm (Mikolov et al., 2013a). Word embedding is a machine learning technique, and Word2vec is known to be relatively fast and accurate (Kwon et al., 2020). The Word2vec algorithm used a skip-gram approach for model training where it started to read each word (i.e., token) from the beginning, reading 10 words before and after the center word together to identify the pattern of common use (Mikolov et al., 2013a). Then, it embedded a chunk of words in a continuous vector space where it mapped semantically similar words to nearby points. We also used cosine similarity to identify the similarity among words in the vector space and control the magnitude effect generated by their occurrences (Mikolov et al., 2013b). Moreover, based on the similar words identified, researchers could find more targeted reviews that contained such words, and the reviews were used as a source of substantive topic modeling.

In topic modeling, topics are distributed over the entire vocabulary used in the collection of reviews, and each review is a mixture of the latent topics (Blei et al., 2003). This process discovers multiple abstract topics over the collection of reviews and provides the probability of topics a review contains. This study used the structural topic model (STM) algorithm (Roberts et al., 2016) in R because it supports a high-processing speed and provides covariates between topics and metadata of documents (Roberts et al., 2014). Identified topics mean the main theme of documents, and they are considered the underlying factors that go through the documents (Kwon et al., 2020).

sections.header_raw: Impact-Asymmetry Analysis: A Three-Factor Theory Approach
sections.header_norm: impact asymmetry analysis a three factor theory approach
paragraphs: Following Mikulić andPrebežac's (2011, 2012) suggestion, we created two sets of dummy variables for each topic to conduct multiple regression analysis. We generated a set of dummy variables by coding a high quantile of topic proportion for each topic as 1 and all the others as 0. Accordingly, we measured the influence on satisfaction in the case of extremely high topic proportion. Likewise, the other set of the dummy variable is obtained by coding a low quantile of topic proportion for each topic as 1, whereas all others are coded as 0. It is used to measure the impact on dissatisfaction in case of a deficient topic proportion. Then, we conducted regression analysis by using the dummy variables as independent variables and satisfaction measured by a reviewer's rating as a dependent variable to generate two Source. Adapted from Kano (1984).

unstandardized coefficients for each topic for a reward index and a penalty index. Drawing on the three-factor theory, comparing two indices leads to categorizing topics into a satisfier, a dissatisfier, or a hybrid. Following Mikulić and Prebežac's (2012) recommendation, we considered a topic a satisfier, a dissatisfier, or a hybrid when the reward index is larger than the absolute value of the penalty index, smaller than the absolute value of the penalty index, or approximately equal to the absolute value of the penalty index, respectively. Table 2 presents a summary of the methods used in this study.

sections.header_raw: Words With Semantic Similarity to Luxury
sections.header_norm: words with semantic similarity to luxury
paragraphs: Using Gensim library (Rehurek & Sojka, 2011) in Python 3.7, this study trained the Word2vec model that read all the words used in text with a chunk of 10 words closely located toward a target word and embedded the word lists in the vector space, having 500 dimensions. The cosine distance among words in two vectors indicates the degree of the semantic similarity of such words. As the cosine similarity is higher, it means that the words are closer in meaning. Considering that no cutoff point exists in terms of the cosine similarity, we reviewed the meanings of identified words and selected the words of which cosine similarities were over 0.44. This study detected the top 21 semantically similar words to "luxury" based on the cosine similarity score through these word embedding vectors, as shown in Table 3.

We classified the selected words into two groups with respect to the conspicuous or inconspicuous aspects of consumption based on the word definition (for definitions of these words, refer to Merriam-Webster, 2021). As mentioned in the literature review, conspicuous consumption is related to displaying social status or financial strength, whereas inconspicuous consumption is related to intrinsic affection or meaningfulness. This dichotomy helped improve insight into customers' perceptions of luxury, although not all the words clearly identified with either conspicuous or inconspicuous consumption. The result exhibits conventional conspicuous aspects (e.g., prestige, privilege, and exclusivity) and inconspicuous aspects (e.g., posh, elegance, sanctuary, and sophistication) of luxury. Furthermore, words related to superfluousness and excessiveness beyond necessity were detected (e.g., extravagance and lavish).

Along with this result, the study selectively found luxury restaurants. Yelp.com provides a price level of each restaurant based on the answers of customers who visited the restaurant. The number of "$" indicates the price level, ranging from "$" to "$$$$" (i.e., "$" = under US $10, "$$" = US$11-US$30, "$$$" = US$31-US$60, and "$$$$" = over US$60), and the amount denotes the average payment per person. Following Table 2.

To identify topics in a luxury restaurant setting Impact-asymmetry analysis To identify satisfiers, dissatisfiers, and hybrids out of topics and examine asymmetric effects on satisfaction Note. Conspicuous = related to displaying social status or financial strengths; Inconspicuous = less signaling and more related to self-pleasure, meaningfulness, or hedonic value. Kim et al. (2006) who regarded a restaurant where an average check per person is more than US$25 as a luxury restaurant, if the price level is over US$30 (i.e., "$$$" or "$$$$"), and more than 5% of the reviews for the restaurant contained the luxury word group above, then the restaurant was considered a luxury one in this study, and the relevant reviews were extracted. Thus, a total of 80,445 reviews (1.36%) from 4,799,240 reviews for 180 restaurants (1.68%) from 13,276 restaurants were finally selected as a representative sample of a luxury setting.

Table 4 provides a summary of the final review data. Cities in California, such as San Francisco (17.06%) and Los Angeles (19.75%), have comparatively large amounts of reviews, followed by New York (14.77%). Reviews were abundant in these areas because of the active posts about restaurant experiences. Regarding price level, 62.78% of restaurants had a price range between US$31 and US$60.

We determined 25 topics by using the metric of semantic coherence and manually investigated keywords over each topic. Then, we selected 12 of them in agreement and excluded those about ethnic food or specific menu due to their irrelevance to our interests. Last, we categorized the factors by inspecting keywords and the review examples that represent each topic. Table 5 shows the selected topics and the naming process used in this study. First, we found that conspicuous consumption or the symbolic value was not explicitly distinct. However, the topic "Impression on other patrons (e.g., dress)" disclosed customers' interests in other customers who visit the same luxury restaurant. This result is consistent with Choi and Mattila's (2016) finding that other customers' dress style in luxury service influences customers' approach behavior. Second, we identified topics regarding gastronomy (i.e., "A variety of course" and "Unique dessert"). Kiatkawsin and Han (2019) observed a close relationship between customers' gastronomic experiences and their visit to luxury restaurants. Third, we found topics regarding aesthetics (i.e., "Great ambience" and "Decorative bar lounge"), which are related to customers' tendency to pursue self-pleasure in an inconspicuous consumption manner (Eckhardt et al., 2015). Fourth, we observed that the topic, "Repetitive visit," reflects the notion that ongoing practice shapes customers' meaning of luxury (Banister et al., 2020). Fifth, we detected the topic, "Special service for an anniversary," which is similar to Hwang and Hyun's (2013) argument that a memorable event in a luxury restaurant at an anniversary provokes strong experience due to the scarcity of the event. Sixth, we found some negative topics about luxury restaurants (i.e., "A mismatched reputation," "Overcharge," "Gross food," and "Wait time"), which reflect the failure of satisfying customers' expectations.

paragraphs: This study explores the factors related to luxury restaurant consumption and the roles of the factors played in customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction. Out of 13,276 restaurants, we identified 180 as luxury restaurants. Given the classification criteria used and the fact that the results represented less than 2% of the restaurants in the original sample, we contended that the study achieved the objective of identifying luxury restaurants. Through topic modeling, 12 topics associated with luxury restaurants were identified. Details will be discussed further in the theoretical implications.

sections.paper_id: Kwon et al. - 2022 - Exploring Customers’ Luxury Consumption in Restaurants A Combined Method of Topic Modeling and Thre
paragraphs: The literature review provided several luxury concepts, indicating the lack of consensus on the definition of luxury. As stated by Yang and Mattila (2016), "where ordinary ends and luxury begins is a matter of degree judged by consumers" (p. 1850). This study contributes to the existing literature by embracing a customer-focused view. We identified 12 significant customer perceptions in the luxury restaurant setting in a holistic view and investigated their roles in formulating customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction.

As previously mentioned, luxury consumption in modern society witnesses the tendency to pursue subjective meaningfulness and self-pleasure, which is called inconspicuous consumption. However, previous hospitality research rarely addressed this phenomenon. The present study explored various factors relevant to inconspicuous consumption in luxury restaurants. The findings revealed that factors concerning customers' gastronomic desire are the most distinct (i.e., four out of 12), thereby revisiting the recent understanding that contemporary customers in the luxury market have inconspicuous consumption habits. Moreover, the form of their inconspicuous consumption should be different from that in non-luxury restaurants. For instance, the required high-level expectation and evaluation may mark specific factors, such as "A variety of course" and "Unique dessert."

Similarly, certain topics, such as "Wait time," "Gross food," and "Repetitive visit," can be considered characteristics of any restaurant. One can argue that if the above customer-perceived topics found in luxury restaurant reviews are also found in reviews for mid-scale restaurants, then the meaning will be different. For example, most luxury restaurants require reservations, and guests expect to have their table upon arrival; thus, a 15-min wait can be viewed as disappointing. In addition, consumers of luxury products place a high value on their time. In mid-scale restaurants, reservations are less common, and wait dissatisfaction is likely due to waiting time exceeding what the host said would be a wait time upon arrival at the restaurant (Kotler et al., 2021).

By contrast, given that the signaling effect of consumption in restaurants is not easily visible, the desire for conspicuous consumption that reflects a social status is revealed subtly. The topic, "Impression on other patrons," implies that a customer sees other customers at the restaurant, which constitutes an essential factor of his or her luxury consumption because other patrons' dress or behavior manifests the social class with which visitors have in common. Bourdieu (1984) identified the similar patterns of cloth wearing and food consumption that people have based on social gratification, distinguishing among social classes. Thus, this topic unveils the hidden appetite for conspicuous consumption in luxury restaurants.

Building on the three-factor theory proposed by Kano (1984), this study extended the one-directional relationship between determinants and customer satisfaction to their dynamic roles as a satisfier, a dissatisfier, and a hybrid. This approach provides an insight into customer behavior about luxury. For example, given that dissatisfiers have a higher propensity for frustration than delight, the findings imply that customers who visit luxury restaurants to pursue Michelin stars can be more disappointed when a flaw occurs than pleased with the customer experience. At best, they may develop feelings close to the acceptable level. The study also offers new insight into how scholars can analyze publicly available data to identify luxury service experiences and customer perceptions.

The literature review indicated that the definition of luxury is subjective and defined by the customer. Chandon et al. (2016) also claimed that luxury brands are becoming common. One implication for future research is to develop a taxonomy of luxury restaurants or other hospitality luxury products, such as hotels. According to different price points, one can group luxury restaurants to see whether these luxury restaurants' consumer perceptions are the same across all groups. Similarly, one can study mid-priced restaurants and investigate the topics consumers used to describe these restaurants, looking for similarities and differences.

By contrast, given that the signaling effect of consumption in restaurants is not easily visible, the desire for conspicuous consumption that reflects a social status is revealed subtly. The topic, "Impression on other patrons," implies that a customer sees other customers at the restaurant, which constitutes an essential factor of his or her luxury consumption because other patrons' dress or behavior manifests the social class with which visitors have in common. Bourdieu (1984) identified the similar patterns of cloth wearing and food consumption that people have based on social gratification, distinguishing among social classes. Thus, this topic unveils the hidden appetite for conspicuous consumption in luxury restaurants.

Building on the three-factor theory proposed by Kano (1984), this study extended the one-directional relationship between determinants and customer satisfaction to their dynamic roles as a satisfier, a dissatisfier, and a hybrid. This approach provides an insight into customer behavior about luxury. For example, given that dissatisfiers have a higher propensity for frustration than delight, the findings imply that customers who visit luxury restaurants to pursue Michelin stars can be more disappointed when a flaw occurs than pleased with the customer experience. At best, they may develop feelings close to the acceptable level. The study also offers new insight into how scholars can analyze publicly available data to identify luxury service experiences and customer perceptions.

The literature review indicated that the definition of luxury is subjective and defined by the customer. Chandon et al. (2016) also claimed that luxury brands are becoming common. One implication for future research is to develop a taxonomy of luxury restaurants or other hospitality luxury products, such as hotels. According to different price points, one can group luxury restaurants to see whether these luxury restaurants' consumer perceptions are the same across all groups. Similarly, one can study mid-priced restaurants and investigate the topics consumers used to describe these restaurants, looking for similarities and differences.

The literature review provided several luxury concepts, indicating the lack of consensus on the definition of luxury. As stated by Yang and Mattila (2016), "where ordinary ends and luxury begins is a matter of degree judged by consumers" (p. 1850). This study contributes to the existing literature by embracing a customer-focused view. We identified 12 significant customer perceptions in the luxury restaurant setting in a holistic view and investigated their roles in formulating customer satisfaction or dissatisfaction.

Moreover, the form of their inconspicuous consumption should be different from that in non-luxury restaurants. For instance, the required high-level expectation and evaluation may mark specific factors, such as "A variety of course" and "Unique dessert."

sections.paper_id: Kwon et al. - 2022 - Exploring Customers’ Luxury Consumption in Restaurants A Combined Method of Topic Modeling and Thre
paragraphs: Luxury restaurants can use the words consumers associate with luxury in their branding and/or communications to express their status. Restaurants can use the impact-asymmetry analysis results (Table 6) as a guide to allocate their assets effectively. For example, a restaurant without a pastry chef can work with a local bakery with a reputation for quality to make some unique signature desserts exclusively for the restaurant. Given that the topic "Unique desserts" is a hybrid factor, luxury restaurants can offer unique desserts to increase customer satisfaction. The resulting satisfaction can pay off in positive WOM, repeat business, and good positioning of the restaurant in consumers' minds. Table 5 shows that doing something special on a special occasion, such as an anniversary, can exceed customers' expectations as a satisfier. Managers of luxury restaurants should ask whether people are dining to celebrate a special occasion to better help them celebrate. One restaurant tastefully sprinkles rose petals on the tablecloth for an anniversary and offers free dessert to congratulate the couple. The cost of providing these extra touches is minimal, especially when most couples spend US$200 or more at the restaurant. Last, the finding that customers have an interest in other customers who visit the restaurant is consistent with the Servuction Model (Hoffman & Bateson, 1997). It supports a luxury restaurant with a dress code to meet minimum standards, and customers feel like they are in a luxury restaurant as they look around the room. It also implies that employee uniforms should be consistent with what one would expect in a luxury restaurant as wearing of such uniforms becomes part of the ambience.

People exert more significant effort to travel to a luxury restaurant than to a casual service restaurant. Thus, luxury restaurants cannot easily identify a competitor as it may be miles away. Through word embedding vectors, this study detected semantically similar words to luxury. Restaurant managers can use the process to recognize their competition in terms of other luxury restaurants and to see whether consumers consider their restaurant as a luxurious one. They may be surprised to find customers' perceptions of them.

Practitioners should know the levels where each attribute is for management and marketing. Satisfied dissatisfiers do not lead to satisfaction, and unsatisfied satisfiers do not lead to dissatisfaction. Luxury restaurants should also be cautious about hybrids because of their nature across both sides of the satisfaction and the dissatisfaction. The findings can help practitioners decide on the strategy to improve or maintain these factors.

sections.paper_id: Kwon et al. - 2022 - Exploring Customers’ Luxury Consumption in Restaurants A Combined Method of Topic Modeling and Thre
paragraphs: This study used Yelp reviews. The website has an even distribution of users in terms of age. Among them, 29% are between 18 and 34 years, 37% are between 35 and 54 years, and 33% are 55 years and older (Yelp, 2020). Yelp users' income does not necessarily belong to an affluent group. Yelp (2020) showed that 52% of their users earn US$100,000 or more, 24% earn US$60,000 to US$99,000, and 25% earn less than US$60,000. Thus, our results may not be generalizable to groups that do not fit Yelp users' demographics. Moreover, we conducted our study in the United States, and the findings may not hold in other regions of the world. Another limitation is derived from analyzing a sheer number of online reviews, which may include some deceptive ones that can be considered a bias. Given that consumers and their behavior are constantly changing, we also acknowledge that our findings may last only for a limited period. Future research can mitigate these limitations by using our methodology to explore other affluent groups and regions outside the United States and conducting longitudinal studies.

The target consumers of luxury hospitality services include affluent, business, and unique occasion customers. Researchers can first survey these consumer groups to find the words they use to describe luxury and then compare these descriptors across groups. Subsequently, scholars can identify the satisfiers, dissatisfiers, and hybrid factors for each group to see whether they vary across groups and determine the implications of this variation.

The identification of the three factors has implications for strategies. Researchers can determine whether these factors remain stable or change over time by conducting a longitudinal study. For example, our results show that doing something special for guests celebrating a special milestone in their lives is a satisfier. Accordingly, future studies can extend this finding by investigating whether this practice will shift from a satisfier to a hybrid or dissatisfier after becoming a common practice in luxury restaurants or whether new satisfiers will evolve.
